failures.situation: Retiree dropped Medicare Part B coverage while living in Portugal, believing private international insurance was a sufficient permanent replacement.
failures.outcome: Upon returning to the US for emergency cardiac treatment 3 years later, faced a 30% permanent Part B premium surcharge (10% per full year without coverage) that cannot be reversed.
failures.cost_range: $1,200-$2,400 per year in additional premiums, compounding indefinitely

failures.situation: Retiree assumed Medicare Advantage plan provided international emergency coverage. Had a stroke in Lisbon and was billed directly by the hospital.
failures.outcome: Medicare Advantage denied the claim entirely — the plan had no foreign coverage. Out-of-pocket hospital bill required negotiation and partial personal payment.
failures.cost_range: $18,000-$45,000 depending on ICU days; typical Portugal private hospital stroke: €12,000-€30,000

failures.situation: Retiree purchased international health insurance without fully disclosing a pre-existing Type 2 diabetes diagnosis — listed it as 'managed condition' without disclosing HbA1c levels or medication history.
failures.outcome: $47,000 emergency hospitalization claim denied in full. Insurer voided the policy retroactively due to material misrepresentation. Retiree had no coverage for 14-month policy period.

failures.situation: Retired couple opened a joint bank account in Portugal without filing FBAR, believing the $10,000 reporting threshold applied per account individually rather than in aggregate across all foreign accounts.
failures.outcome: IRS audit 2 years later triggered FBAR willful violation assessment. The couple's combined foreign accounts exceeded $200,000. Assessment applied 50% of account peak balance.
failures.cost_range: $40,000-$100,000 in penalties plus $8,000-$15,000 in tax attorney fees

failures.situation: American retiree in Mexico failed to report a Mexican investment account holding mutual funds, believing it was below the $10,000 threshold after partial withdrawals. Peak balance during the year was $32,000.
failures.outcome: FBAR threshold applies to peak balance at any point during the calendar year, not the balance at year-end. Non-willful penalty of $10,000 per violation per year assessed.
failures.cost_range: $10,000-$30,000 in penalties for 2-3 unfiled years; typically resolved via Streamlined Filing Compliance Procedures
failures.source: r/ExpatFinance and expat CPA community reports

failures.situation: American retiree applied for Portugal NHR tax status believing US Social Security benefits would be tax-free under the regime.
failures.outcome: Under the US-Portugal tax treaty, Social Security benefits remain taxable in the US regardless of Portuguese tax status. NHR provided zero benefit for this income type. Retiree had paid Portuguese and US taxes simultaneously, requiring amended returns.
failures.cost_range: $2,000-$5,000 in unnecessary tax advisor fees; overpaid Portuguese taxes requiring amended returns at $1,500-$3,000 in professional fees
failures.source: Expat tax professional community reports and r/PortugalExpats

failures.situation: American retiree applied for Portugal D7 visa with proof of Social Security income of $1,800/month, having read the minimum requirement on an expat blog that was 12 months out of date.
failures.outcome: Visa denied at Portuguese consulate in the US. The minimum passive income requirement had been updated and the retiree's income fell below the current threshold. Lost non-refundable application and translation fees.
failures.cost_range: $800-$2,500 in application fees, certified translations, and accommodation during consulate appointment; full re-application required

failures.situation: Retiree submitted D7 visa application with bank statements showing sufficient income but the statements were older than 3 months at the time of the consulate appointment (appointment was rescheduled twice).
failures.outcome: Application rejected for expired documentation. Required gathering fresh statements and rescheduling, adding 3-4 months to the process.
failures.cost_range: $400-$800 in additional document procurement and notarization fees; 3-4 month additional delay

failures.situation: Retiree attempted to wire $95,000 in home sale proceeds from US bank account to newly opened Mexican bank account to purchase property.
failures.outcome: US bank flagged transaction under Suspicious Activity Report protocols. Funds held for 6 weeks. Required notarized documentation of property transaction, source of funds letter, and consultation with a US banking compliance attorney.
failures.cost_range: $500-$1,200 in legal and notarization fees; 6-week delay caused them to miss the property closing window

failures.situation: Retiree closed US checking account after establishing Portuguese bank account, then discovered their Social Security direct deposit and IRS refund had no valid US account to deposit into.
failures.outcome: SSA suspended direct deposit and issued paper checks to a US address that was no longer monitored. Three months of benefits went uncollected. IRS refund check was lost in mail.
failures.cost_range: $4,500-$7,500 in delayed SSA payments; SSA reinstatement took 6-8 weeks; IRS refund recovery required filing Form 3911

failures.situation: American retirees purchased property in coastal Mexico (within 50km of the coast) directly in their personal names, not through a fideicomiso (bank trust) as required by Mexican law for foreign nationals.
failures.outcome: Property rights challenged. Transaction voided. Required re-purchasing through a fideicomiso with associated setup and annual trust fees.
failures.cost_range: $3,000-$6,000 in fideicomiso setup; $500-$1,200/year in annual trust fees; legal fees $5,000-$12,000 to resolve the title issue
failures.source: Mexico real estate attorney community reports

failures.situation: Retiree with 20 years in a state government job (with its own pension and no Social Security deductions) retired abroad without accounting for the Windfall Elimination Provision. Expected $2,100/month in SS benefits.
failures.outcome: WEP reduced Social Security benefit to $890/month — a permanent $1,210/month reduction that cannot be appealed if WEP applies correctly.
failures.cost_range: $14,520/year in permanently reduced benefits; $145,200 over 10 years

failures.situation: American retiree in Portugal assumed SNS registration was automatic upon receiving residency permit. Did not register as a utente at the local health center for 18 months.
failures.outcome: 18 months without access to subsidized prescription medications and specialist referrals. Full private rates paid for all care.
failures.cost_range: $200-$600/month in excess healthcare costs; specialist visits €80-€200 each vs. near-free with SNS
